content.text: Trigonometry has numerous practical applications in real-world scenarios involving the measurement of heights and distances. This chapter explores how trigonometric ratios can be used to determine heights of towers, buildings, poles, and distances between objects without direct physical measurement.

The fundamental concepts in this chapter are the line of sight, angle of elevation, and angle of depression. These concepts form the basis for solving all height and distance problems using trigonometry.

content.title: Line of Sight
content.text: The line of sight is the straight line drawn from the eye of an observer to the point on the object being viewed. It represents the direct visual connection between the observer and the object of interest.

The line of sight is always straight and passes through the observer's eye to the specific point being observed. In diagrams representing height and distance problems, the line of sight is typically represented as a line connecting the observer's position to a point on the object (such as the top of a tower or the bottom of a building).

content.title: Key Observation
content.text: The line of sight forms the hypotenuse of the right-angled triangle in height and distance problems. The observer's eye, the object being viewed, and the horizontal reference point form the three vertices of this triangle.

content.title: Angle of Elevation
content.text: The angle of elevation is the angle formed between the line of sight and the horizontal line when the object being viewed is above the horizontal level. In other words, it is the angle through which the observer must raise their head (or line of sight) to look at the object.

When we look up at an object that is higher than our eye level, we form an angle of elevation. This angle is measured from the horizontal line upward to the line of sight. Common examples include looking at the top of a tower from the ground, or viewing the top of a building from a distance.

In a right-angled triangle formed by the observer, the object, and the horizontal distance, the angle of elevation is one of the acute angles. If we denote this angle as θ, then the trigonometric ratios can be directly applied to find unknown sides using the known angle and known distance.

content.title: Understanding Angle of Elevation
procedure: Stand at a distance from a tall structure (building, pole, tower).
Look straight ahead at the horizontal level.
Now look at the top of the structure by raising your head.
The angle your line of sight makes with the horizontal line is the angle of elevation.
Try this from different distances and observe how the angle changes.
content.observation: As you move closer to the structure, the angle of elevation increases. As you move farther away, the angle of elevation decreases.
content.conclusion: The angle of elevation depends on both the height of the object and the horizontal distance of the observer from the object.

content.title: Angle of Depression
content.text: The angle of depression is the angle formed between the line of sight and the horizontal line when the object being viewed is below the horizontal level. In other words, it is the angle through which the observer must lower their head (or line of sight) to look at the object.

When we look down at an object that is lower than our eye level, we form an angle of depression. This angle is measured from the horizontal line downward to the line of sight. Common examples include a person on a building looking at an object on the ground, or a person on a bridge looking at something below.

content.title: Important Relationship
content.text: If an observer at point A looks at point B with an angle of depression θ, then an observer at point B looking at point A will see an angle of elevation θ. This is due to the properties of alternate interior angles when a transversal crosses two parallel lines (the horizontal lines through A and B).

In problems involving angles of depression, we use the same trigonometric ratios as we do for angles of elevation. The right-angled triangle is formed in the same way, with the angle of depression being one of the acute angles.

To solve height and distance problems using trigonometry, we follow a systematic approach: (1) Draw a clear diagram representing the problem, (2) Identify the right-angled triangle, (3) Mark the known quantities (distances, angles), (4) Identify what needs to be found, (5) Choose the appropriate trigonometric ratio, (6) Solve the equation to find the unknown.

content.title: Choosing the Right Trigonometric Ratio
content.text: To choose the correct trigonometric ratio, identify: (a) the side opposite to the known angle, (b) the side adjacent to the known angle, (c) the hypotenuse. Then use sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse, cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse, or tan θ = opposite/adjacent based on which sides are known and which is to be found.

content.title: Finding Height of a Tower (Angle of Elevation)
content.problem: A tower stands vertically on the ground. From a point on the ground which is 15 m away from the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is 60°. Find the height of the tower.
solution: Let AB represent the tower with height h, and C be the observation point 15 m away from B.
The angle of elevation ∠ACB = 60°.
In right triangle ABC (right-angled at B), we have AB (opposite side), BC = 15 m (adjacent side), and the angle is 60°.
Using tan ratio: tan 60° = AB/BC
Therefore, the height of the tower is 15√3 m.

content.title: Finding Length of Ladder (Angle with Horizontal)
content.problem: An electrician needs to reach a point 3.7 m up a 5 m pole. If the ladder is inclined at 60° to the horizontal, what is the length of the ladder required, and how far from the pole should the ladder be placed?
solution: Let BC be the ladder inclined at 60° to the horizontal, with B at the point on the pole 3.7 m high.
BD = 3.7 m (vertical height to reach), and ∠BCD = 60° (angle with horizontal).
To find BC (length of ladder): Using sin 60° = BD/BC
To find DC (distance from pole): Using cot 60° = DC/BD
Therefore, the ladder length is approximately 4.28 m and should be placed 2.14 m from the pole.

content.title: Finding Height Using Observer's Height
content.problem: An observer 1.5 m tall is 28.5 m away from a chimney. The angle of elevation of the top of the chimney from her eyes is 45°. What is the height of the chimney?
solution: Let AB be the chimney, and CD be the observer of height 1.5 m.
The observer's eye is at height E = 1.5 m, and CE = 28.5 m (horizontal distance).
Let AE = h (height of chimney above the observer's eye).
From the right triangle AEC with angle of elevation 45°:
tan 45° = AE/CE
Total height of chimney = AE + 1.5 = 28.5 + 1.5 = 30 m
Therefore, the height of the chimney is 30 m.

content.title: Finding Height with Two Different Angles of Elevation
content.problem: From a point P on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of a 10 m tall building is 30°. A flag is hoisted at the top, and the angle of elevation of the top of the flagstaff from P is 45°. Find the length of the flagstaff and the distance of the building from P. (Take √3 ≈ 1.732)
solution: Let AB = 10 m (building height), BD = x (flagstaff length), and PA = distance from P to building.
From right triangle PAB with angle 30°:
tan 30° = AB/PA
From right triangle PAD with angle 45° where AD = AB + BD = 10 + x:
tan 45° = AD/PA
Therefore, the flagstaff length is approximately 7.32 m and the distance is 17.32 m.

content.title: Finding Height Using Shadow Length at Two Different Sun Altitudes
content.problem: The shadow of a tower on level ground is 40 m longer when the sun's altitude is 30° than when it is 60°. Find the height of the tower.
solution: Let AB = h (height of tower), BC = x (shadow length at 60° altitude), and DB = x + 40 (shadow length at 30° altitude).
From triangle ABC with angle 60°:
tan 60° = h/x
From triangle ABD with angle 30°:
x = 20 m
Therefore, the height of the tower is 20√3 m.

topics.topic_name: Angles of Depression in Different Configurations

content.text: Angles of depression are particularly useful when an observer is at a height and needs to determine distances or heights of objects below. The problem may involve looking at two different points from the same elevated position, requiring us to set up multiple equations simultaneously.

content.title: Finding Height and Distance Using Two Angles of Depression
content.problem: The angles of depression of the top and bottom of an 8 m tall building from the top of a multi-storeyed building are 30° and 45° respectively. Find the height of the multi-storeyed building and the distance between the two buildings.
solution: Let PC be the multi-storeyed building, AB be the 8 m tall building, and AC be the distance between them.
From the property of alternate interior angles: ∠PBD = 30° and ∠PAC = 45°.
In right triangle PBD: BD = PD/tan 30° = PD√3
In right triangle PAC: AC = PC/tan 45° = PC
Since AC = BD and PC = PD + DC where DC = AB = 8 m:
PC = PD + 8
Also, AC = BD, so: PC = PD√3
Therefore: PD + 8 = PD√3
Distance between buildings = AC = 4(3 + √3) m
Therefore, the height of the multi-storeyed building is 4(3 + √3) m and the distance is 4(3 + √3) m.

content.title: Finding River Width Using Angles of Depression from Bridge
content.problem: From a point on a bridge across a river at height 3 m above the banks, the angles of depression to the banks on opposite sides are 30° and 45° respectively. Find the width of the river.
solution: Let P be the point on the bridge at height 3 m, and A and B be points on opposite banks.
DP = 3 m where D is the foot of the perpendicular from P.
Angle of depression to bank A is 30°, so ∠PAD = 30°.
Angle of depression to bank B is 45°, so ∠PBD = 45°.
In right triangle APD: tan 30° = DP/AD
AD = 3√3 m
In right triangle BPD: tan 45° = DP/BD
BD = 3 m
Width of river AB = AD + BD = 3√3 + 3 = 3(√3 + 1) m ≈ 8.196 m
Therefore, the width of the river is 3(√3 + 1) m.

topics.topic_name: Mixed Problems: Combining Angles of Elevation and Depression

content.text: Some problems involve both angles of elevation and angles of depression simultaneously. These problems require careful diagram construction and may involve solving systems of equations. The observer might be at an intermediate height, looking both upward and downward at different objects.

content.title: Finding Tower Height Using Both Angles
content.problem: From the top of a 7 m high building, the angle of elevation of the top of a cable tower is 60°, and the angle of depression of its foot is 45°. Determine the height of the tower.
solution: Let AB be the cable tower, and the observer is at point C on a 7 m high building.
From C, angle of elevation to A (top of tower) is 60°.
From C, angle of depression to B (foot of tower) is 45°.
Let the distance from building to tower be d.
From the angle of depression 45°: tan 45° = 7/d, so d = 7 m
Let height of tower be h. Then AB = h.
The height of A above C is (h - 7) meters.
From the angle of elevation 60°: tan 60° = (h - 7)/d
Therefore, the height of the tower is 7(1 + √3) m.

glossary.definition: The straight line drawn from the eye of an observer to the point on an object being viewed; it represents the direct visual connection between observer and object.
The angle formed between the line of sight and the horizontal line when the object is above the horizontal level; measured upward from the horizontal.
The angle formed between the line of sight and the horizontal line when the object is below the horizontal level; measured downward from the horizontal.
An imaginary line passing through the observer's eye perpendicular to the direction of gravity; serves as the reference line for measuring angles of elevation and depression.
The ratio of sides of a right-angled triangle; includes sine (sin), cosine (cos), and tangent (tan) which relate angles to side lengths.
Equal angles formed when a transversal crosses two parallel lines; used to relate angles of elevation and depression when horizontal lines are parallel.
The person or point from which observations are made regarding heights, distances, and angles to other objects.
The longest side of a right-angled triangle, opposite to the right angle; represents the line of sight in height and distance problems.
The side of a right-angled triangle that is next to a given acute angle but is not the hypotenuse; often represents horizontal distance.
The side of a right-angled triangle that is across from a given acute angle; often represents vertical height in height and distance problems.

summary_points: Applications of trigonometry include solving real-world problems involving heights and distances of objects that cannot be measured directly.
The line of sight is the straight line from the observer's eye to a point on the object being viewed.
The angle of elevation is the angle between the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is above the eye level (looking upward).
The angle of depression is the angle between the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is below the eye level (looking downward).
Right-angled triangles formed in height and distance problems allow us to apply trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) to find unknown heights or distances.

When solving height and distance problems: (1) Draw a clear diagram, (2) Identify the right-angled triangle, (3) Mark known values, (4) Select appropriate trigonometric ratio, (5) Solve for the unknown.
The observer's height must be considered when the observer is not on the ground; the height of the observer is added to or subtracted from calculated values depending on the configuration.
Angles of elevation and depression from different positions to the same object can be combined to solve for multiple unknowns using systems of equations.
Alternate interior angles theorem is useful: if an observer at A looks down at B with angle of depression θ, an observer at B looking at A sees angle of elevation θ.
Common trigonometric values used in problems: sin 30° = 1/2, cos 30° = √3/2, tan 30° = 1/√3; sin 45° = cos 45° = 1/√2, tan 45° = 1; sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = 1/2, tan 60° = √3.
